nodes.name: adjective
nodes.context: Adjectives tune nouns: the swift knight, a circumspect player. Precision instruments, not decoration.

nodes.name: adverb
nodes.context: Adverbs tune verbs (and adjectives): moved carefully, extremely sharp. Many wear the -ly badge; the sneaky ones (very, quite, never) do not.

nodes.name: conjunction
nodes.context: Conjunctions join: and, but, or, because, although. The hinges every compound and complex sentence swings on.

nodes.name: noun
nodes.context: Nouns name things: people, places, objects, ideas. The first sorting job — everything a sentence talks ABOUT is a noun or acts like one.

nodes.name: preposition
nodes.context: Prepositions place things in space and time: under the board, before the storm, across town. They start little location- phrases that glue onto sentences.

nodes.name: pronoun
nodes.context: Pronouns stand in for nouns: he, she, it, they. Each points back at something — a broken pointer (unclear antecedent) is the classic bug.

nodes.name: verb
nodes.context: Verbs are the engine: actions (run, build) and states (is, seems). No verb, no sentence — the one part of speech a sentence cannot live without.

nodes.name: complete
nodes.context: Complete sentence = subject + predicate that can stand alone. Fragments are missing a piece ('because the storm came'); run- ons are two sentences taped together without a hinge. Diagnosis and repair — the editor's first skill.

nodes.name: object
nodes.context: The object receives the action: the knight took THE ROOK. Ask the verb 'what?' — the answer is the object. Subject-verb- object is English's default word order; scrambling it is what other languages (and bad sentences) do.

nodes.name: predicate
nodes.context: The predicate is everything the sentence SAYS about the subject — the verb plus its cargo. From Latin prae + dicare, 'to proclaim': what is proclaimed about the subject. 'The knight | took the rook': subject, then predicate. Every complete sentence splits into these two pieces.

nodes.name: subject
nodes.context: The subject is WHO or WHAT the sentence is about — find the verb, ask who is doing it. In 'the knight took the rook', the subject is the knight.

nodes.name: complex
nodes.context: Complex sentence: an independent clause plus one or more dependent ones. Where writing stops sounding like a list and starts sounding like thinking.

nodes.name: compound
nodes.context: Compound sentence: two independent clauses hinged with a conjunction (and, but, so) or a semicolon. Two complete thoughts, one sentence, equal partners.

nodes.name: dependent
nodes.context: A dependent clause has a subject and verb but leans on the main sentence ('although he was winning, ...'). The leaning word (although, because, when) is what makes it lean.

nodes.name: independent
nodes.context: An independent clause is a complete sentence living inside a bigger one — it could stand alone if you cut it free.

nodes.name: blending
nodes.context: Fuse isolated phonemes into a word: /s/ /t/ /o/ /m/ /p/ -> 'stomp'. Oral only. The decoding-direction twin of segmenting.

nodes.name: elision
nodes.context: Delete or swap a phoneme and say what remains: 'smile' without /s/ -> 'mile'. The strongest single indicator of phonemic mastery; drill deliberately.

nodes.name: segmenting
nodes.context: Break a spoken word into its individual phonemes: 'ship' -> /sh/ /i/ /p/. Oral only, no letters. The substrate skill for all encoding; weakness here surfaces as poor spelling and decoding of novel words.

nodes.name: substitution
nodes.context: Swap one phoneme for another and say the result: change /k/ in 'cat' to /b/ -> 'bat'. Hardest pure-sound skill; fluency here makes novel spellings feel computable.

nodes.name: syll count
nodes.context: Count syllables in spoken words by clapping or chin-drops. Aural only. Every syllable has exactly one vowel sound — the bridge from sound to written syllable division.

nodes.name: ai
nodes.context: ai spells /ā/ mid-word (train, paint); ay takes over at word end. Position decides the spelling.

nodes.name: au aw
nodes.context: au (mid-word: haunt, sauce) and aw (word end: draw, hawk) spell /aw/. Same position logic as ai/ay, oa/ow.

nodes.name: ay
nodes.context: ay spells /ā/ at word end (play, stray); ai takes the middle (train). Position decides — assess the ai/ay choice explicitly.

nodes.name: ea
nodes.context: ea usually spells /ē/ (beach, dream) but sometimes short /e/ (bread, head) — first vowel team with ambiguity; teach as probabilities, not exceptions.

nodes.name: ee
nodes.context: Vowel team ee spells /ē/ (street, speech, sweep).

nodes.name: ie
nodes.context: ie spells /ī/ at word end (pie, tie) and often /ē/ mid-word (field, chief). The 'i before e' territory — teach the reliable core, flag the exceptions as loanwords.

nodes.name: igh
nodes.context: igh spells /ī/ (light, fright, high). Three letters, one sound — a fossil cluster from Old English where gh was once pronounced.

nodes.name: oa
nodes.context: Vowel team oa spells /ō/ (coach, toast, groan).

nodes.name: oo
nodes.context: oo has two sounds: long (moon, scoop) and short (book, stood). First team where the reader must try both — teach as probabilities.

nodes.name: ow long
nodes.context: ow spells /ō/ at word end (snow, grow, elbow) while oa takes the middle (coach). Another position pair like ai/ay.

nodes.name: acronyms
nodes.context: Words with birth certificates: laser (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation), scuba, radar, NASA — acronyms said as words — versus initialisms spelled out (FBI, USA, DVD). Some acronyms became so wordlike we forgot (laser takes -s and lowercase). A word can be BORN as a sentence.

nodes.name: civic
nodes.context: Everyday civic shorthand: Co. (company), Inc., Ltd., St., Ave., Dr., Mr./Mrs. (mister/missus), and the two-letter state codes (CT, NY, CA) — a complete little geography cipher. Reading the world's signage and envelopes.

nodes.name: compression
nodes.context: The unifying idea: every medium invents its own shorthand because writing costs something — chisel strokes (SPQR carved in Rome), telegram fees (STOP), thumb-typing (lol). Compression is a constant of language; only the medium changes. Given a shorthand, infer its medium and era. iykyk and i.e. are the same invention, twenty centuries apart.

nodes.name: internet
nodes.context: The newest compression layer: lol, brb, fyi, asap, tbh, imo, iykyk ('if you know you know'). Some predate the internet (FYI and ASAP are office memo speak). The real skill is REGISTER: lol belongs in a chat with friends and never in an essay — knowing which language fits which room is a language skill, not a rule of politeness.

nodes.name: latin abbrev
nodes.context: Latin fossils in everyday text: i.e. (id est, 'that is'), e.g. (exempli gratia, 'for example'), etc. (et cetera, 'and the rest'), ergo ('therefore'), vs (versus), AM/PM (ante/post meridiem), AD, and even lb (libra). Knowing the expansion ends the i.e./e.g. confusion forever — they are different Latin sentences.

nodes.name: ew ue
nodes.context: ew (chew, threw) and ue (blue, rescue) spell /ū/ or /yū/ at word end. Completes the long-u spelling inventory with u_e and oo.

nodes.name: oi oy
nodes.context: The /oy/ diphthong: oi mid-word (coin, moist), oy at word end (boy, enjoy). Position pair, fully regular — a rule that never betrays you.

nodes.name: ou ow
nodes.context: The /ow/ diphthong: ou mid-word (loud, count), ow at end or before n/l (cow, town, howl). A gliding two-part vowel — the mouth moves during it.

nodes.name: closed
nodes.context: A syllable ending in a consonant has a short vowel (rab-bit, nap-kin). First and most common syllable type.

nodes.name: cle
nodes.context: Consonant-le: final syllable like -ble, -dle, -tle (ta-ble, pud-dle, lit-tle). Count back three and divide; a preceding short vowel needs a doubled consonant.

nodes.name: division
nodes.context: Split multisyllable words at the seam (VC/CV: nap-kin; V/CV: ro-bot) and spell each chunk with its own rules. Segmenting at the syllable level — the multiplier skill for long words.

nodes.name: open
nodes.context: A syllable ending in a vowel has a long vowel (mu-sic, ro- bot). Contrast with closed.

nodes.name: rctrl
nodes.context: R-controlled syllables (gar-den, cir-cus): the vowel-r unit stays glued through division. Fifth syllable type.

nodes.name: schwa
nodes.context: Schwa: the unstressed 'uh' that any vowel letter can make (about, wagon, pencil). Why sounding-out fails in unstressed syllables — spell those by morpheme and by sight instead.

nodes.name: silent e
nodes.context: Vowel-consonant-e makes the vowel long and the e silent (plane, stripe, quote). The e is doing a job, not decoration.

nodes.name: vteam
nodes.context: Vowel-team syllables keep the team together when dividing (rain-bow, foot-ball). Fourth syllable type.

nodes.name: air are ear
nodes.context: The /air/ family: air (chair), are (share), ear (bear) — and ear's second life as /ear/ (hear). Ambiguity managed by word sets and frequency.

nodes.name: ar
nodes.context: Bossy r: ar spells /ar/ (shark, garden). The r captures the vowel — neither long nor short. First r-controlled pattern.

nodes.name: er ir ur
nodes.context: The three spellings of /er/: er (her, fern), ir (bird, first), ur (turn, burst). Sound alone cannot decide — er is the safest guess (most common), ir/ur are learned sets.

nodes.name: or
nodes.context: or spells /or/ (storm, fort); ore and oar variants (shore, roar) join later reading.

nodes.name: arabic
nodes.context: The Arabic layer came through science and trade: algebra (al- jabr, 'the reunion'), algorithm (al-Khwarizmi's name), zero (sifr), plus coffee, cotton, sugar, magazine. The mathematics words are Arabic because the mathematics was — for centuries Baghdad was where math lived.

nodes.name: eastern
nodes.context: Trade-route and empire loans from the East: shampoo, jungle, bungalow, pajamas (Hindi/Urdu), checkmate and bazaar and paradise (Persian), tea and ketchup (Chinese dialects), tsunami, karate, tycoon (Japanese). Every word is a shipping lane.

nodes.name: fossils
nodes.context: Silent-letter fossils: kn (knight), wr (wrench), gn (gnome), mb (thumb) were all once pronounced. The spelling preserved the older word — the silent letters are Old English archaeology.

nodes.name: french ch
nodes.context: ch = /sh/ marks recent French arrivals (chef, machine, parachute). Completes the ch decision: pick the sound by the word's origin and register.

nodes.name: german modern
nodes.context: Modern German loans — different from the ancient shared bones: kindergarten ('children's garden'), rucksack, pretzel, hamburger (from Hamburg — no ham involved), wanderlust. Compare with germanic_core to feel the difference between inheritance and borrowing.

nodes.name: germanic core
nodes.context: English's bones are Germanic: house/Haus, water/Wasser, bread/Brot, finger/Finger. The 100 most common words are almost all Old English. Cognates with modern German are family resemblance, not borrowing — the two languages are siblings.

nodes.name: greek ch
nodes.context: ch = /k/ marks Greek (chorus, echo, chemistry, stomach). The ch trio: /ch/ native, /k/ Greek, /sh/ French — one digraph, three passports.

nodes.name: greek ph
nodes.context: ph = /f/ marks a Greek word (phone, graph, physics). Given a word is Greek, PREDICT the f-sound spelling; given ph, know its passport.

nodes.name: italian
nodes.context: The Italian layer is music and food: piano (short for pianoforte, 'soft-loud'), opera, solo, tempo, pizza, spaghetti ('little strings'), broccoli ('little arms'). Music notation is written in Italian worldwide — a whole profession's vocabulary from one country.

nodes.name: latin direct
nodes.context: Renaissance scholars borrowed straight from Latin, giving English TRIPLES — one idea, three registers by origin: kingly (Old English) / royal (French) / regal (Latin); ask / question / interrogate. Register is etymology you can feel.

nodes.name: layers
nodes.context: English is a three-layer code: Anglo-Saxon (short, everyday: bread, house), Latin/French (official, layered: nation, court), Greek (science: photo, chorus). The layer explains the spelling — classification, not memorization.

nodes.name: loanwords
nodes.context: Loanwords keep their passports: checkmate (Persian shah mat, 'the king is dead'), shampoo (Hindi), ketchup (Hokkien), robot (Czech), chocolate (Nahuatl), tsunami (Japanese), safari (Swahili from Arabic). 'Irregular' usually means 'immigrant' — the spelling follows the source language's rules.

nodes.name: norman french
nodes.context: 1066: Norman French becomes the language of power. The class split survives in food — the animal in the field is Anglo- Saxon (cow, pig, sheep — the farmer's words) but the meat on the table is French (beef, pork, mutton — the lord's words). Court, judge, army, royal: the vocabulary of rule is French.

nodes.name: norse
nodes.context: The Viking layer: Norse settlers gave English sky, skin, skull, egg, knife, window — and even the pronouns they/them. Many sk- words are Norse. A conquest you can hear a thousand years later.

nodes.name: spanish
nodes.context: The Spanish layer maps the American West: canyon (cañón), rodeo, ranch, mosquito ('little fly'), tornado, patio, vanilla. Spanish also carried Nahuatl words north — chocolate and tomato are Aztec words wearing Spanish passports. Two-hop immigration.

nodes.name: timeline
nodes.context: The whole story in order: Germanic bones -> Viking layer -> 1066 French -> Renaissance Latin and Greek -> global trade and empire -> modern borrowings. Given any word's origin, place it on the timeline and the map. English never rejected a word it could use — that is why the code has layers, and why the layers explain the spellings.

nodes.name: able ible
nodes.context: -able and -ible both say 'can be'; the base picks the vowel. A word that stands alone usually takes -able (accept-able, remark-able); a bound Latin stem takes -ible (poss-ible, vis-ible, respons-ible). The family is the tiebreak: responsible -> responsibility, never responsability. The most misspelled suffix pair in English — regular once it's a morpheme decision instead of a sound decision.

nodes.name: compounds
nodes.context: Compound words: two whole words glued (base+ball, rain-bow, air-port). Each part keeps its spelling — divide between the words, not by syllable mechanics.

nodes.name: derive
nodes.context: The superpower node: given an UNSEEN word, crack meaning and spelling from owned morphemes — contradict = contra (against) + dict (say) = speak against. Assessment is always novel words. This is vocabulary by inference; it never runs out.

nodes.name: families
nodes.context: Word families: respond -> response -> responsible -> responsibility. One base, many suits — spelling travels through the family. This is the anti-schwa move: when an unstressed vowel is murky, walk to a relative that says it clearly (defin-ite leans on fin-ite; compos-i-tion on compos-ite). Assessment: given one member, produce and spell the relatives.

nodes.name: prefixes
nodes.context: Prefixes are fixed-spelling units glued to a base: un-, re-, pre-, dis-, mis-. Spelling by assembly, not memory — morphology as algebra.

nodes.name: prefixes 2
nodes.context: Second prefix set with meanings: sub- (under), inter- (between), trans- (across), non- (not), over-, under-. Prefix meanings are stable cargo — sub+marine, inter+state, trans+port.

nodes.name: roots greek
nodes.context: Greek combining forms: tele (far), graph (write), meter (measure), phon (sound), photo (light). They combine freely: tele+phone = far sound; photo+graph = light writing. Science vocabulary is Greek Lego.

nodes.name: roots latin 1
nodes.context: First Latin roots with meanings: struct (build), port (carry), dict (say), form (shape). Words become equations: con (with) + struct = build together; ex (out) + port = carry out; pre (before) + dict = say before.

nodes.name: roots latin 2
nodes.context: Second Latin set: tract (pull), spect (look), ject (throw), rupt (break). At-tract = pull toward; in-spect = look into; e-ject = throw out; inter-rupt = break between; circum-spect = looking around = careful.

nodes.name: segment
nodes.context: Take whole words APART: pre|cognition, respond|er, un|help|ful. The analysis mirror of building — the same segmenting instinct as the sound layer, one level up. Cut at morpheme seams, not syllable beats (re|sponse, never res|ponse); every piece must mean something, and the learner says what each piece means.

nodes.name: suffix simple
nodes.context: No-change suffixes: -ly, -ful, -ness, -less, -er, -est attach without altering the base (hope+ful, quick+ly). Meaning is compositional: hopeful = full of hope.

nodes.name: suffixes
nodes.context: The integration node: vowel vs consonant suffixes and which base-change rule each triggers (hope->hoping, hop->hopping, carry->carried, hope->hopeful). Construction under load — every rule below fires here.

nodes.name: tion sion
nodes.context: -tion/-sion turn verbs into nouns and spell /shun/ (act->action, discuss->discussion). The single most common 'irregular' sound-spelling — regular once you know it's a morpheme.

nodes.name: contractions
nodes.context: Contractions: the apostrophe marks exactly the deleted letters (do not -> don't, it is -> it's). Surgical, not decorative — and the it's/its distinction falls out for free.

nodes.name: doubling
nodes.context: The 1-1-1 rule: one syllable, one vowel, one final consonant -> double before a vowel suffix (hop -> hopping vs hope -> hoping). Pure if-then logic; teach it as an algorithm.

nodes.name: doubling 2syll
nodes.context: Doubling in two-syllable words: double only if the FINAL syllable is stressed (begin->beginning, but visit->visiting). The 1-1-1 rule plus stress — an if-then with two conditions.

nodes.name: e drop
nodes.context: Drop silent e before a vowel suffix (make -> making, hope -> hoping); keep it before a consonant suffix (hopeful).

nodes.name: k c ck
nodes.context: Choosing the /k/ spelling: c before a/o/u or consonants (cab, clip), k before e/i/y (kite, keep), ck only right after a short vowel (duck). Integrates ck + soft c into one decision tree.

nodes.name: plural f
nodes.context: f plurals: many f/fe words voice to ves (leaf->leaves, knife->knives); some don't (roofs, chiefs). A learned set with a strong tendency.

nodes.name: plural s es
nodes.context: Plurals: add s, but es after s, x, z, ch, sh (boxes, wishes) — you can hear the extra syllable. Sound tells you which.

nodes.name: plural y
nodes.context: y plurals: vowel+y just adds s (boys); consonant+y changes y to i and adds es (parties). Same y->i machinery as suffixes.

nodes.name: soft c
nodes.context: c says /s/ before e, i, y (city, race, cycle) and /k/ everywhere else (cat, clip). A completely reliable if-then — the letter after c decides.

nodes.name: soft g
nodes.context: g says /j/ before e, i, y (giant, gem, gym) — usually. Same trigger as soft c but with holdouts (get, give); teach as strong tendency, not law.

nodes.name: y to i
nodes.context: Change y to i after a consonant when adding a suffix (carry -> carried) unless the suffix starts with i (carrying).

nodes.name: ch
nodes.context: ch spells /ch/ at word start or after anything but a short vowel (chess, crunch, punch).

nodes.name: ck
nodes.context: Positional rule: right after a short vowel, /k/ is spelled ck (block, stick, check). Elsewhere plain k or c.

nodes.name: dge
nodes.context: Third member of the short-vowel family: right after a short vowel, /j/ is spelled dge (badge, fudge, bridge); elsewhere ge (cage, hinge).

nodes.name: ph
nodes.context: ph spells /f/ (graph, phone, trophy). A Greek birthmark — every ph word came from Greek, which is why it exists alongside plain f. Links forward to the etymology region.

nodes.name: qu
nodes.context: q never travels alone: qu spells /kw/ (quiz, quest, squint). Treat qu as one tile.

nodes.name: sh
nodes.context: Two letters, one phoneme: sh spells /sh/ anywhere in a word (shift, brush, splash).

nodes.name: tch
nodes.context: Positional rule paired with ck: right after a short vowel, /ch/ is spelled tch (catch, pitch, stretch); after anything else, ch (bench, coach). Classic confusion point — assess the contrast explicitly.

nodes.name: th
nodes.context: th spells both voiced and unvoiced /th/ (think, month, cloth).

nodes.name: wh
nodes.context: wh spells /w/ in question words and a few others (whisk, wheat).

nodes.name: blends
nodes.context: Encode initial and final consonant clusters (st-, dr-, -mp, -st): every sound still gets its own letter, they just crowd together. Distinct from digraphs, where letters merge into one sound.

nodes.name: cvc
nodes.context: Encode consonant-vowel-consonant words from speech: map each phoneme to a single letter in order. Short vowels only.

nodes.name: floss
nodes.context: The FLOSS rule: after a short vowel in a one-syllable word, final f, l, s (and z) double — stiff, hill, pass, buzz. First taste of position-driven doubling.

nodes.name: glued
nodes.context: Glued (welded) sounds: -ng and -nk families (ring, thank) and all/am/an. The vowel and consonants fuse; teach as units, not letter-by-letter.

nodes.name: short vowel
nodes.context: Recognize the five short vowel sounds and that a single vowel followed by a consonant in a closed syllable is short. Gatekeeper concept for ck/tch/dge and doubling rules.
